turns.content: I've been reading about the Read-Copy-Update pattern but I still don't get how it actually works in Rust without blocking readers.

The core idea is that readers never block. Instead of holding a lock, you create a new copy of the data structure when you need to modify it. Readers continue operating on the old version while writers work on the fresh one.
So I just keep two versions around? But how do I manage which readers see which version without some kind of synchronization?

You use an epoch-based garbage collection system. Each thread maintains a local epoch counter that increments every time it starts reading new data, while the writer tracks the global epoch to know when to swap.
That sounds complicated with all these counters and what about how I actually implement the read in my code?

You typically use Arc to share ownership of your data structure across threads. When a thread starts reading, it clones its local copy of the pointer using Arc::clone so multiple readers can access the same immutable snapshot without blocking.
Ah okay with Arc but I don't understand how the epoch counter actually gets synchronized between threads to know when to update?
The writer increments a global atomic counter using atomics and stores that value in the shared data structure along with a pointer. Readers compare their local epoch against this stored value.
So if my reader's epoch is behind what's written, it means I need to update? And how do I actually perform that update safely?

Once a reader detects its epoch is stale, it starts reading from the new snapshot. The writer then swaps the pointer by creating a new Arc-wrapped version of the data and updating the global counter atomically.
Wait so I'm still holding onto an old Arc while the writer creates a new one? That seems like there could be memory leaks if readers never notice?
Exactly, that's why you need to track when readers finish. You can implement this by having threads periodically check their epoch against the global one and release old Arcs when they fall behind.
I think I'm getting closer but I still don't see how this actually avoids blocking readers in practice compared to a simple mutex?

With a mutex, every read operation waits for the writer to finish. With RCU using Arc and epoch counters, reads are truly fire-and-forget because they just load the pointer value without waiting for any lock.
So readers can start immediately while writers work? But what happens when I actually try to implement this in my code right now?

You declare a shared Arc pointer and an atomic epoch counter. Your reader function reads the stored pointer, clones it with Arc::clone if needed, and checks the epoch. The writer atomically swaps the data while incrementing the counter.
This is starting to click but I'm still confused about how exactly the atomic swap works without blocking anyone?
The atomic pointer swap happens on a separate storage slot that tracks which Arc points to the data. Writers use atomics to update this slot while readers only read it, so there's no contention.
Okay so I have my reader checking epochs and writer doing atomic swaps but what about cleanup when threads exit or finish reading?
When a thread finishes its work, it drops the Arc clone which releases memory. The epoch tracking ensures you don't accidentally hold onto old data that's no longer valid.
Wait so I should be checking my local epoch against the global one continuously? And when they match I can safely use the data?
Yes, that's exactly it. Your reader loads the current pointer and its stored epoch value, compares them with your local counter, and only uses the data once they align.
I'm trying to understand the difference between using Arc::MUTABLE and Arc::SHARED for a shared cache in my service, but I keep getting confused about when to use one versus the other.

It's actually quite common to mix these up when first tackling RCU patterns. The core distinction is that Arc::MUTABLE requires exclusive access because it holds an immutable borrow of the data inside, whereas Arc::SHARED allows multiple threads to read concurrently without blocking each other. In a true Read-Copy-Update scenario where you want readers to never block, you must use Arc::SHARED so that your read phase doesn't acquire any locks.

Okay, but I'm still stuck on how the atomic pointer swap fits in here since my current implementation seems to be blocking writes when threads try to read during a write operation. It feels like I'm doing something wrong with the synchronization primitives.

That's exactly the misconception you're hitting. The RCU pattern specifically relies on an epoch-based garbage collection strategy where readers are never blocked by writers. You don't acquire any locks for reading; instead, you use atomic pointer swaps to transition from one epoch to another and then discard old data via a finalizer callback. Your current blocking behavior suggests you might be using std::sync::Mutex or similar primitives when you should be relying on the atomic nature of Arc pointers themselves.

I see now that I was trying to protect access with a Mutex, but in RCU we don't do that at all because the Arc pointer swap is lock-free and safe for concurrent reads. So the key insight is that readers just follow pointers until they hit a stale epoch marker?

Exactly right. The magic happens when you combine atomic pointer swaps with Arc::clone semantics to create an immutable snapshot of data that readers can safely examine without any synchronization overhead. Writers simply copy their new state into a fresh Arc and swap the global pointer atomically, while old pointers remain valid for existing readers until they're collected.

That makes sense conceptually, but I'm still worried about memory safety since we're not using explicit locks to prevent data races between writers and readers. Is there something specific about how Rust handles this with the standard library?

Rust's ownership model combined with the RCU pattern ensures safety because Arc::SHARED guarantees that only one mutable reference exists at any time, preventing concurrent mutations of the same data structure. The atomic pointer swap ensures writers never see stale pointers during their critical section since they're swapping before finishing their work, and readers are guaranteed to see either a complete old state or a complete new state.
So I should refactor my code to use Arc::SHARED for the shared cache and implement an epoch counter that gets swapped atomically when updating? And then maybe add a finalizer callback for cleanup?

That's perfect. You'll want to track your current epoch number, increment it on every write, swap the atomic pointer using std::sync::atomic::{AtomicUsize}, and attach a finalizer function that runs when old pointers are dropped. This ensures you can safely discard stale data without blocking any readers.
I'm working on a side project this weekend and I think I can just use Arc<Mutex<T>> to handle concurrent reads without any extra complexity.

That's a common starting point, but Mutex actually blocks readers entirely while the lock is held. For high-throughput scenarios you want something where readers never block and writers only update when they need to.
I've seen RCU mentioned in some blogs so I think it's just a fancy name for Mutex but with different locking logic?
No, RCU is fundamentally different because readers don't wait at all. Writers perform an atomic pointer swap using Arc::clone to create a new epoch while readers continue using the old one.
So if I have a shared data structure and multiple threads reading it, they will just keep accessing the old version forever?
Exactly. Readers follow the pointer to their current epoch without any synchronization overhead. Only writers perform the atomic update that creates a new epoch.
This sounds like it could solve my memory management problem since I'm worried about stale data in my side project.
You can combine RCU with an epoch-based GC to reclaim old epochs when they're no longer referenced. The key is using Arc::clone for the epoch pointers and atomic operations for the swap itself.
I'm not sure how I would implement this since my current code uses std::sync::{Arc, Mutex} everywhere.
You need to replace the mutex pattern with a read-mostly structure. Use Arc<T> for the data and an atomic counter or similar mechanism to track epoch transitions without blocking readers.
Wait, so I can just keep my existing code mostly intact? I don't have to rewrite everything to use RCU?
You only need to change how you expose the shared state. Keep your data structure as is but wrap it with an epoch-based access pattern so readers never wait.

I need to port a high-throughput data pipeline from C++ to Rust, but I'm worried about the shared memory overhead. Can you explain how RCU with Arc::clone and atomic pointer swaps actually avoids blocking readers while still allowing safe concurrent updates?

The core idea is that writers never block readers; readers are never blocked either because they only read a copy of the data without holding any locks. You start by having an epoch-based GC where you keep a list of old epochs, and when a writer finishes an update, it atomically swaps a pointer to a new version using atomic pointer swap while simultaneously incrementing an epoch counter that readers check before accessing memory. The key is Arc::clone because it shares ownership without copying the actual data until needed,

Wait, so I'm still allocating new buffers every time? That sounds like O(N) space complexity and defeats the purpose of low latency. I thought RCU was about zero-copy semantics since C++ uses RCU for that too.

You are correct to question it; you assumed that every swap creates a full copy which is expensive, but in Rust with Arc::clone we only clone the metadata and reference count increases without duplicating the underlying data buffer until it's actually accessed. The real zero-copy magic happens because readers see the old epoch until the writer finishes its atomic pointer swap to the new version,
But if I use Arc::Weak for the epoch counter, won't that cause double-free panics when the strong count drops? I've seen that happen in my C++ port where weak pointers fail unexpectedly.

You made a mistake there; you assumed Arc::Weak is sufficient for tracking epoch lifetimes without considering how Rust's memory safety rules differ from raw pointers. The correct approach uses atomic u64 for the epoch counter combined with Arc::Strong for the data, and you never actually need to store an explicit weak reference because the epoch lifecycle is guaranteed by the atomic swap operation itself,
I don't get it; if I just use a simple Mutex around my shared buffer, why would anyone bother with all this complexity when it looks like more code for less safety?

You are right to feel confused because you assumed that locking is the only way to ensure thread safety in Rust. The RCU pattern is specifically designed to eliminate lock contention entirely by decoupling reads and writes, so your pipeline can handle millions of concurrent requests without any blocking,
Okay then tell me exactly how I should implement this in my data structure right now since I'm still thinking about the epoch management.

You are overthinking it; you need to define a shared buffer type that implements Arc and an atomic counter for epochs, then write a writer function that atomically swaps the pointer and increments the epoch while creating a new copy of the data only when necessary. The reader function simply checks if the current epoch matches its cached epoch before accessing memory,
